headline: Lakota and Cheyenne Prepare for Battle, 1876
body:
The sun, a relentless eye in the vast Montana sky, beat down upon the encampment along the Greasy Grass, known to the white man as the Little Bighorn River. It was June 24, 1876, a day heavy with anticipation, a day of profound spiritual preparation for the Lakota and Cheyenne. The air thrummed not with fear, but with a resolute determination, a quiet strength born of generations defending their sacred lands and way of life. Thousands had gathered, a vibrant city of tipis stretching across the valley, families and elders alongside the warriors, all united in the face of an encroaching storm. The U.S. Army, under the command of Lieutenant Colonel George Armstrong Custer, was drawing near, its intent clear: to break the spirit of resistance that pulsed through this great gathering.

Weeks prior, a powerful vision had been granted to Tatanka Iyotake, Sitting Bull, the revered Hunkpapa Lakota leader. During a sacred Sun Dance ceremony, a ritual of prayer, fasting, and profound sacrifice, he had offered 50 pieces of flesh from each arm, a testament to his unwavering devotion to Wakan Tanka, the Great Mystery. In the throes of this spiritual communion, a vision had descended upon him: soldiers, countless as grasshoppers, falling headlong into the Lakota camp, their bodies inverted. This was not merely a dream; it was a prophecy, a divine assurance of victory. A voice, clear and resonant, had whispered that these grasshoppers had no ears, a potent symbol of the soldiers' inability to comprehend or heed the cries for freedom and sovereignty from the Native peoples. This vision, shared among the tribes, had ignited a fierce hope, a conviction that triumph over the bluecoats was within their grasp.

On this day, June 24, the encampment was a hive of activity, yet imbued with a solemn purpose. Warriors engaged in ceremonies, some performing the 'dying dancing ceremony,' taking vows to fight until their last breath, their commitment to their people and their land absolute. The sounds of daily life mingled with the low chants and rhythmic drums of spiritual rites. The sheer scale of the gathering was immense, an estimated 7,000 to 10,000 souls, with 1,500 to 2,000 warriors, a formidable force united by a common cause. They were not merely preparing for battle; they were preparing to defend their very existence, their culture, their future. The events of this day were not just a prelude to a battle; they were a powerful affirmation of identity, a collective act of spiritual and physical readiness for the decisive conflict that would unfold on the morrow. The sun dipped below the horizon, casting long shadows over the valley, but the spirit of the Lakota and Cheyenne remained unyielding, illuminated by the vision of their revered leader. As Sitting Bull himself once declared, "Let us put our minds together to see what life we can make for our children."
location: Little Bighorn River, Big Horn County
